text: In this video, we describe our three techniques to promote erectile preservation during male robotic cystectomy. We begin with an intrafacial nerve sparing cystectomy.

The surgeon makes a medial incision in the posterior rectovesicle peritoneum to develop a medial space, followed by mobilization of the seminal vesicles and vasa, resembling the monsurus approach used in prostatectomy.

The surgeon places retraction on the seminal vesicles and incises the fold of Denonvilliers fascia at the apex. The pedicle to the seminal vesicles is controlled using either Hem-o-lok or metallic clips, and the intrafascial plane is identified.
The surgeon dissects the intrafascial plane distally and laterally, controlling vessels entering the base of the prostate medially, completing the development of the medial space.
We now describe our technique for prostate capsule sparing without sparing of the seminal vesicles. Men are required to undergo PSA and MRI testing prior to the procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the seminal vesicles and vasa, developing a medial space. The prostatic capsule is sharply incised above the fold of the Denonvilliers fascia, entering a plane between the central and peripheral zones of the prostate. The peripheral zone is visually identified by its yellow appearance.

The surgeon continues dissection in the identified plane, using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques to extend the dissection distally and laterally. A thirty-degree lens may be utilized to enhance the view, especially in cases with small prostates. The dissection progresses towards the apex of the prostate.
The surgeon develops the lateral plane as far as possible, completing the medial space development before proceeding with the cystectomy.

The surgeon performs prostate capsule sparing while preserving the seminal vesicles. A medial space is developed above the level of the seminal vesicles and vasa, with the prostate capsule incised at the entry point of these structures into the prostate, forming the ejaculatory duct.

The surgeon identifies the plane by the yellow appearance of the peripheral zone. Using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, the peripheral zone is separated posteriorly from the central and transitional zones anteriorly. As the dissection progresses, the ejaculatory ducts become visible in the midline, confirming the correct plane, and are subsequently divided. The vasa are also divided, serving as a landmark for further dissection.

The surgeon develops the lateral bladder spaces after establishing the medial space. The vasa are divided, and dissection proceeds towards the endopelvic fascia. Peritoneal incisions are joined, leaving a strip of peritoneum over the ureter for use in ureteric anastomosis. The ureter is clipped and divided.

The surgeon divides the bladder pedicles using haemlock clips and a robotic vessel sealer, ensuring dissection occurs below the ureter to avoid bladder entry. The dissection is performed above the seminal vesicle and the previously divided vas, minimizing disturbance to the neurovascular bundles, and continues until reaching the base of the prostate.

The surgeon completes the lateral spaces and releases the bladder from its anterior attachments. Medial retraction of the prostate is performed, and a lateral plane is developed. Vascular structures are divided high on the prostate, and the process is repeated on the contralateral side.

The surgeon defats the anterior prostate, exposing the intact puboprostatic ligaments. A distal plane is developed before focusing on the dorsal venous complex (DVC), where a sub-DVC dissection is performed to minimize bleeding. The apical dissection continues until the urethra is reached. The catheter is clipped to prevent urine spillage and then cut, with any remaining attachments being divided.

The surgeon places the excised specimen into a retrieval bag. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) is over-sewn, showing the appearance after sparing the prostate capsule and seminal vesicles, with intrafascial nerve sparing.

Between two thousand and seventeen and two thousand and twenty two, we performed forty one cases of prostate capsule sparing cystectomy and thirty four cases of nerve sparing cystectomy. Prostate cancer was identified in only twenty four percent of cases where capsule sparing was performed, the majority of which was Gleason six. In our experience, men undergoing prostate capsule sparing had superior erectile functional recovery and were less likely to require assistance with erections.
